title: Mint jelly
subtitle: Cooking apples, mint, vinegar, sugar.
excerpt: Bramley apples cooked down with water and white vinegar, strained through a jelly bag overnight, then boiled with sugar and a generous infusion of fresh mint to a clear gold-green set. The lamb companion. Makes four 200 ml jars.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), 'Mint Jelly'. The two-stage extract method matches her instructions exactly: pectin pulled from windfall cooking apples, then cleared and set with sugar and herb.

content.text: Bramley apples, skins and cores included, simmered in water and white wine vinegar until they collapse. The pulp tipped into a muslin-lined sieve over a bowl, then left to drain overnight. The next morning, the clear apple liquid is boiled with sugar and a big bundle of mint to setting point, strained again, and jarred.

The two-stage method gives a sparkling clear jelly with no apple solids. The mint goes in at the boil so the flavour stays bright; chopped mint added at the end keeps the green colour but cooks bitter. Use the cleanest apples you have: a windfall or two is fine, but bruise marks bleed brown into the jelly.

items.name: Bramley apple
items.prepNote: windfall is fine; chop roughly, skins and cores included

items.name: Granulated sugar
items.prepNote: weight to be calculated from the strained liquid yield (see method)

items.name: Mint
items.prepNote: a large bunch (~30 g) plus a smaller bunch for finishing

content.text: Roughly chop {{apple-bramley}} of Bramley apples without peeling or coring. Tip into a stockpot with {{water}} of water, {{white-wine-vinegar}} of white wine vinegar, and the large bunch of mint stems (save the leaves for the boil). Cover and simmer for 45 minutes until the apples collapse into pulp.
or muslin-lined sieve over a deep bowl. Tip the apple pulp in. Cover and leave overnight, 8 to 12 hours. Don't squeeze or stir the bag, or the jelly cloudies.
Weigh the liquid and the sugar
Measure the strained liquid. For every 600 ml, weigh out 450 g of granulated sugar. Tip the liquid into a jam pan, set on a low hob, and stir in the sugar until fully dissolved.
Boil to setting point with mint

Add a small bundle of fresh {{mint}} of mint leaves tied with kitchen twine. Bring to a rolling boil. Boil 10 to 15 minutes until the temperature hits 104.5°C, or a teaspoon dropped on a chilled saucer wrinkles when pushed. Fish out the mint bundle. Skim any green foam.
Rest the pan 5 minutes. Stir in 2 tablespoons of very finely chopped fresh mint leaves for colour. Ladle into sterilised jars. Seal warm.

items.fix: Always let gravity do the work overnight. Cloudy jelly tastes the same but doesn't catch the light on the shelf.
Re-boil with the cold-saucer test guiding the end. The ratio of 450 g sugar to 600 ml apple liquid is the safe rule.
items.cause: Sugar ratio off or didn't reach setting point.

items.fix: Add the finishing chopped mint off the heat. Store in a dark cupboard.
items.cause: Mint boiled too long or jelly stored in direct light.

items.fix: Tied bundle in, fished out; chopped leaves added only off the heat for colour.

content.text: Mint jelly is the British lamb companion, sat on the Sunday-roast table in a glass dish with a small spoon. It also works alongside cold cured meats, on cheese with a sharp cheddar, or warmed and brushed over grilled lamb chops as a glaze.
